coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of two-dimensional CeO2-δ-GQD composites and their structural and optical properties
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Santana-Hernandez, S.
Hernandez-Perez, M.A.
Villanueva-Ibáñez, M.
Chen, F.
Shen, Q.
Tu, R.
Vargas-Garcia, J.R.
Zhang, L.M.
coredata.dc:description: The present study reports on the synthesis of two-dimensional (2D) ceria and graphene quantum dots (CeO2-δ-GQD) composites and their functional properties. Composites were prepared by a simple liquid phase deposition (LPD) method followed by a thermal treatment in air at 250 and 400 °C. The structural and optical properties of composites were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD), Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM), High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM), Raman spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV-Vis and photoluminescence (PL). The composites consist of crystalline CeO2-δ regions of about 1.8–2.9 nm in size, coexisting with highly disordered GQDs areas. The CeO2-δ-GQD composites experience gradual dehydration and oxidation of carbon processes during thermal treatment, influencing the CeO2 crystal size, Ce3+/Ce4+ ratio, and O vacancies presence. These effects allow for the control of some of the composite functional properties. Specifically, the CeO2-GQD250 composites exhibit a low recombination rate of charge carriers due to the synergistic interaction between the components and low presence of Ce3+. In contrast, the CeO2-GQD400 composites show a high Eg of 3.8 eV (Eg ∼3.2 eV for CeO2NPs) associated with a CeO2 quantum size effect and extremely low fraction of GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Composites
Ceria
Graphene quantum dots
Oxygen vacancies
Quantum size effect

Santana-Hernandez acknowledges "CONACYT", "México" the financial support for doctoral studies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of pristine GQDs and CeO2-δ-GQD composites, (b) Curve-fitting analysis in the range 2θ = 5–40° for pristine GQDs and CeO2-δ-GQD composites.
FESEM images of pristine GQDs and CeO2-δ-GQD composites.
HR-TEM images of (a-c) pristine GQDs, (d-f) CeO2-GQD250 composites.

(a) Raman spectra of pristine GQDs and CeO2-δ-GQD composites, (b) Characteristic F2g band in the composites, (c) Curve-fitting analysis of the first-order of the C region, (d) Curve-fitting analysis of the second-order of the C region.
FT-IR curve-fitting analysis of pristine GQDs and CeO2-δ-GQD composites.
XPS high-resolution spectra of pristine GQDs and CeO2-δ-GQD composites, (a) C 1 s (b) O 1 s, (c) Ce 3 d core levels for CeO2-δ-GQD composites.
(a) UV–Vis Absorption spectra of pristine GQDs, CeO2-δ-GQD composites and CeO2NPs, (b) Tauc’s curves and Eg values of pristine GQDs, CeO2-δ-GQD composites and CeO2NPs.
(a) PL spectra of pristine GQDs, CeO2-δ-GQD composites and CeO2NPs excited at 320 nm, (b) PL spectrum of CeO2-GQD250 composites excited from 300 to 370 nm.
CeO2 crystallite size and XPS analysis of CeO2-δ-GQD composites.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of two-dimensional CeO2-δ-GQD composites and their structural and optical properties

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International School of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan 430070, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present study reports on the synthesis of two-dimensional (2D) ceria and graphene quantum dots (CeO2-δ-GQD) composites and their functional properties. Composites were prepared by a simple liquid phase deposition (LPD) method followed by a thermal treatment in air at 250 and 400 °C. The structural and optical properties of composites were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD), Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM), High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM), Raman spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV-Vis and photoluminescence (PL). The composites consist of crystalline CeO2-δ regions of about 1.8–2.9 nm in size, coexisting with highly disordered GQDs areas. The CeO2-δ-GQD composites experience gradual dehydration and oxidation of carbon processes during thermal treatment, influencing the CeO2 crystal size, Ce3+/Ce4+ ratio, and O vacancies presence. These effects allow for the control of some of the composite functional properties. Specifically, the CeO2-GQD250 composites exhibit a low recombination rate of charge carriers due to the synergistic interaction between the components and low presence of Ce3+. In contrast, the CeO2-GQD400 composites show a high Eg of 3.8 eV (Eg ∼3.2 eV for CeO2NPs) associated with a CeO2 quantum size effect and extremely low fraction of GQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last decade, special attention has been paid to developing cerium oxide (CeO2) with two-dimensional (2D) anisotropic nanostructure because of its unusual properties, especially for the increased surface oxygen vacancies that promote catalytic activity [1–4]. The CeO2 is an advantageous lanthanide oxide with main applications in catalysis, fuel cells, and sensors derived from its high oxygen storage capacity and easy Ce4+/Ce3+ transition [5,6]. The non-layered nature of CeO2 hinders the progress toward its easy and controllable preparation with 2D anisotropic nanostructure [7]. Then, diverse strategies have been adopted to prepare 2D-CeO2 and 2D-CeO2-composites, promoting their exceptional properties. Essentially, these strategies consist in modifying the “know-how” of wet chemical synthesis or alternatively the use of structural templates. For instance, numerous papers have reported the synthesis of CeO2 nanosheets by soft precipitation method from Ce(NO3)36H2O and NH4HCO3 and subsequent thermal treatment [8–10]. Hydro and solvothermal methodologies have also been profusely reported for obtaining 2D CeO2 materials [11,12]. On the other hand, the use of biotemplates such as rose petals [13] and leaves [14] have been reported to grow 2D CeO2 structures. However, graphene as a natural 2D material additionally provides high electrical conductivity, high mobility of charge, and unique optical properties. The use of graphene is a promising strategy that has been investigated to obtain CeO2 nanoparticles [15], nanoflakes [16–18], and nanosheet-structured microflowers [19]. Significant results from CeO2/graphene studies suggest that synthesis conditions influence the interaction between the graphene surface and Ce chemical precursor, which in turn defines the growth of CeO2. The appropriate mixture of graphene and Ce-precursor solutions before CeO2 formation has been reported as being effective for growing nanosheets of CeO2. For example, Lijun Lei, et al. developed CeO2 nanosheets by mixing graphene oxide (GO) sheets and Ce nitrate precursor in ethanol solutions [20]. Tong Li, et al., prepared CeO2 nanocrystals by using reduced GO combined with Ce nitrate precursor in acrylamide/dimethylformamide solution [21]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be regarded as a valuable option for improving the functional properties of composites. Recently, titania-ceria-GQD nanocomposites were synthesized by the hydrothermal method and showed benefits in their electrical and optical properties [22]. In this study, we demonstrate a facile, low-temperature, and low-cost LPD method to obtain novel 2D CeO2-δ-GQD composites. As an innovative interaction scenario between the GQDs surface and the Ce nitrate precursor, the GQDs were used in the pristine state. The effects of the thermal treatment on CeO2 crystal size, Ce3+/Ce4+ ratio, and O vacancies presence are analyzed.

The CeO2-δ-GQD composites were synthesized by LPD method using GQDs (∼99.00 % XF001H) as one of the components and Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (99.999 % Sigma- Aldrich) as Ce precursor. The NaOH (≥97.00 % Meyer) was used as an oxidizing agent. The GQDs were used in pristine conditions without functionalization treatment. First, GQDs were mixed with Ce precursor in a molar ratio of 1:1 for 60 min in an ultrasonic bath (35 kHz). Then, the 0.5 M NaOH solution was slowly added under magnetic stirring until pH 11. The resultant suspension was filtered, and the dark-brown solid was repeatedly washed with deionized water and dried in an open atmosphere at about 80 °C using an infrared lamp. The dried product was collected and labeled as CeO2-GQD80 composites. Subsequently, they were subjected to thermal treatment at 250 and 400 °C in air for 30 min using a heating rate of 5 °C/min. The products were named CeO2-GQD250 and CeO2-GQD400 composites.

XRD patterns of the composite powders were recorded utilizing a Rigaku MiniFlex 600 diffractometer in the Bragg-Brentano (θ-2θ) geometry with Cu Kα (λ = 1.5405 Å) radiation and HyPix 400 MF (2D HPAD) detector operated in two-dimensional mode, working at 45 kV and 15 mA. The scan range (2θ) covered from 5° to 90° with a step width of 0.02° and a constant counting time of 0.6 s/step. A silicon zero background sample holder was employed. The PDXL software from Rigaku was employed for phase identification. All XRD patterns were fitted into Gaussian profiles using the software OriginPro 2018 to separate overlapping peaks. The background was removed by subtracting the region below a straight line that connects the two lowest points at about 2θ = 5° and 40° on the curve pattern. Such points were determined by the zero value of the 2nd horizontal derivative. The refinement showed a good fit between the observed and calculated patterns. The correlation coefficients were typically < 0.99. The software provided a list of accurate peak parameters in terms of peak height, peak width, and peak position.

The morphology of composites was investigated by FESEM (JEOL JSM6701F) at an accelerating voltage of 5 kV using the secondary electron detector. The FESEM samples were prepared by placing the composite powders on Cu tapes and sputter-coated with Au-Pd.

The size and distribution of composite components were examined by HR-TEM with a cold-field emission JEOL JEM-ARM200CF microscope, operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV in the bright-field mode. The HR-TEM samples were prepared by dispersing the composites in isopropyl alcohol by ultra-sonication for 60 min. Then, a drop of the suspension was placed on a carbon-coated Cu grid and evaporated under ambient conditions.

The Raman spectra were collected using a Jobin Yvon Horiba 800 spectrometer coupled to a confocal optical microscope. The laser excitation wavelength employed was 532 nm, and the laser spot diameter was about 15 µm.
The FT-IR analysis was carried out by an Agilent Technologies Cary 630 spectrometer, operated in attenuated total reflection (ATR) mode, with a ZnSe crystal.

The XPS analysis was performed on a Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer equipped with a 180° double-focusing hemispherical analyzer and a monochromatic Al Kα (1486.6 eV) source. The calibration was carried out with the binding energies (BEs) of Au 4 f7/2 (84.00 eV) and Cu 2p3/2 (932.67 eV). The measurement resolution was 0.4 eV considering the full-wide at half maximum (FWHM) of Ag 3d5/2. Survey and high-resolution spectra of composite powders were collected at 160 eV (step size of 1.0 eV) and 40 eV (step size of 0.1 eV), respectively. The XPS spectra were analyzed using the software Avantage from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. The spectra fitting was based on the Shirley-type background subtraction, pseudo-Voigt function with L/G mix product of 0.3 [23], and FWHM of graphene oxide C 1 s (1.5 eV) [24,25]. The figure-of-merit, Chi-square was 0.15. The BEs in the XPS spectra were calibrated by setting the C 1 s peak of adventitious carbon at 284.8 eV.

The UV-Vis spectra were recorded using a double-beam Perkin Elmer Lambda 35 spectrometer in transmission mode from 1100 to 200 nm. The analysis was carried out on representative composite films deposited by an “interfacial technique”. A suspension was formed by adding 3 mg of CeO2-δ-GQD composites in deionized water/toluene solution (1:1 vol) and stirring for 24 h. Just at the end of the stirring time, a glass substrate was immersed. Then, a uniform composite film was formed at the water/toluene smooth interface. Subsequently, the substrate was carefully taken out from the bottom and the composite film easily adhered to its surface. The PL analysis was performed using a Horiba Scientific Fluorolog FL3–11 instrument with a 450 W short-arc xenon lamp and excitation source between 300 and 600 nm. The samples were prepared by dispersing the composite powders in deionized water.

Fig. 1a shows XRD patterns of pristine GQDs and CeO2-δ-GQD composites. The XRD pattern of pristine GQDs displays three broad peaks centered at about 2θ = 23.5°, 50.3°, and 81.4°, which were attributed to (002), (004), and (006) planes of the graphite structure (JCPDS 041-1487). The characteristic (002) peak for GQDs has been previously reported at 2θ values ranging from 22° to 25° due to lattice distortion produced by the presence of numerous oxygen functional groups [25–27]. In contrast, XRD patterns of CeO2-δ-GQD composites exhibit broad diffraction peaks associated with the cubic fluorite planes of the CeO2 structure (JCPDS 034–0394). In these patterns, no diffraction peaks for GQDs are distinguished (Fig. 1a). The CeO2-δ-GQD composite structure was analyzed by an XRD profile fitting. Fig. 1b contains the curve-fitting results at the 2θ range between 5° and 40°. The analysis suggests the clear presence of the (001) plane of oxygenated graphite at GQDs and composites. Thus, the composites contain a mixture of graphitic carbon, Ce(OH)3, CeO2, and Ce2O3. Further, composites exhibit a higher contribution of graphitic carbon [(001) and (002) peaks] as a result of heating at 400 °C. This suggests selective oxidation of the more disordered carbon. The apparent crystallite size, L, of CeO2 was estimated from the high intensity (111) and (200) peaks using the Scherrer´s equation,
L =
K λ
β cos Θ
, where K is Scherrer constant, K = 0.86 [28], β is the broadening (in radians), and θ is the Bragg angle. The crystallite size of the CeO2 component varies between 2.1 and 2.7 nm at CeO2-δ-GQD composites ( Table 1).

The FESEM images of pristine GQDs and CeO2-δ-GQD composites are presented in Fig. 2. The GQDs show the typical graphene wrinkled-sheet morphology with a planar size of several micrometers. The CeO2-δ-GQD composites conserve basically the wrinkled-sheet morphology in which the size of sheets decreases with temperature. The HR-TEM images in Figs. 3a-c and 3d-f display intrinsic structural features of GQDs and CeO2-GQD250 composites, respectively. Both materials exhibit a few-layer sheet nature. The fast Fourier transform (FFT) image in Fig. 3c shows that GQDs exhibit well-resolved (002) lattice fringes (region B) with d spacing of 0.34 nm of graphite (002) planes coexisting with disordered-lattice areas (region A). Region A displays two sets of spots of hexagonal patterns suggesting overlapped graphene layers with relative rotation and tilt angles [29]. In contrast, region B shows only two brighter spots. When observed in a planar view by HR-TEM, the graphene wrinkled-sheet morphology might explain the coexisting effect of (002) lattice fringes altogether with overlapped graphene layers. The FFT image in Fig. 3f indicates that CeO2-GQD250 composites exhibit similar well-resolved lattice fringes (region C) but with a d spacing of 0.31 nm, corresponding to CeO2 (111) planes. Thus, composites consist of CeO2 crystalline areas integrated into overlapped graphene layers (region A). Region C displays two sets of spots suggesting a slight misorientation of CeO2. The HR-TEM observations indicated that the crystallite size of CeO2 varied from 1.8 nm to 2.9 nm, as summarized in Table 1. The (111)-oriented nature of the oxide suggests that individual CeO2 nanosheets have grown on the starting graphene sheets with their [111] direction parallel to the c-axis of the graphene. The CeO2 follows an isotropic growth mode and evolves into equiaxed nanoparticles (NP) in the absence GQDs layered template. The CeO2NPs were used as reference material for optical measurements.

Fig. 4a illustrates the Raman spectra of pristine GQDs and CeO2-δ-GQD composites. The GQDs display characteristic graphite first- (D and G) and second-order (G´) bands at 1345 cm−1, 1590 cm−1, and 2906 cm−1, respectively. The D, G, and G´ bands at CeO2-δ-GQD composites gradually decrease with the increase in temperature. Raman spectra of composites exhibit, in addition, the distinctive F2g band of CeO2 at about 458 cm−1. The position of the G band at CeO2-GQD80 composites is upshifted from 1590 to 1600 cm−1 with respect to pristine GQDs. This implies a charge transfer process in which electrons are transferred from pristine GQDs to Ce cations (Ce4+→Ce3+). No change in the position of the G band is observed between CeO2-GQD80 and CeO2-GQD250 composites. In the opposite way, the G band of CeO2-GQD400 composites is downshifted from 1600 to 1582 cm−1 when compared with CeO2-GQD80 composites. The electrons are transferred from Ce cations to remainders of GQDs (Ce3+→Ce4+) at CeO2-GQD400 composites [30]. The intensity ratios ID/IG for CeO2-GQD80 and CeO2-GQD250 composites are about 0.64 and 0.72, which indicate a similar disorder level as compared with pristine GQDs (0.64). This information is consistent with the high level of disorder revealed by HR-TEM observations at GQD areas (region A in Fig. 3c and 3f). However, the ID/IG for CeO2-GQD400 of about 0.19 indicates the dominant presence of C sp2 hybridization, i.e. less level of disorder. The intensity of first-order stretching vibration of the Ce−O bond, F2g, increases with temperature, as shown in Fig. 4b, in agreement with the increase of CeO2 revealed by XRD analysis. The position of the F2g band at CeO2-GQD400 composites is downshifted from 458 to 453 cm−1, as compared to CeO2-GQD80, suggesting an increase in oxygen vacancy concentration in the former [31]. This is congruent with the comparatively higher intensity of D1 and D2 bands at CeO2-GQD400 composites, which are typically associated with intrinsic oxygen vacancies at the CeO2 structure [32].

The crystallite size of CeO2 was also estimated from the Raman F2g peak broadening using the equation,
Γ
cm
- 1
= 10 +
124.7
D R
, where Γ is the FWHM of the Raman F2g active mode and DR is the crystallite size [33]. The crystallite size increased with temperature from 2.5 to 3.4 nm, in good agreement with XRD and HR-TEM results (Table 1). To elucidate the structural characteristics of GQDs at composites, Raman spectral analysis was performed following the deconvolution models proposed by Chun-Zu Li et al. [34,35]. First- (800–2000 cm−1) and second-order (2200–3400 cm−1) regions were fitted separately with Gaussian-Lorentzian function using an iteration procedure to obtain the best combination of a multi-band system as shown in Fig. 4c and 4d. According to deconvolution models, first-order spectra of sole GQDs and both CeO2-GQD80 and CeO2-GQD250 composites were adequately fitted with a combination of ten bands. The S, D, VR, VL, GR, and G bands emerged as the main bands, while R, SR, SL, and GL represent curve-fitting residuals. The VR, VL, and GR bands suggest the presence of amorphous carbon and small-size aromatic domains of 3–5 rings. Besides, SR and GL bands may be associated with carbonyl groups CO and C–H of aromatic and benzene (ortho-di-substituted) rings, respectively. While all ten bands are present at pristine GQDs and CeO2-GQD80 and CeO2-GQD250 composites, exclusively SR, D, G, and GL bands prevailed at CeO2-GQD400. This indicates distinctive graphitic features among composites. In particular, CeO2-GQD400 composites exhibit small graphitic domains with comparatively higher order and less presence of oxygenated functional groups compared with CeO2-GQD80 and CeO2-GQD250 composites, consistent with XRD results. In the second-order Raman spectra Fig. 4d, the curve fitting analysis shows the 2 S, 2D, 2 VR, and 2 G bands, which correspond to the overtones of the first-order S, D, VR, and G bands. The presence of such overtones corroborates the analyzed characteristics of first-order results.

The structural chemistry of pristine GQDs and composites was studied by deconvolution of the FT-IR spectra. Fig. 5 shows the curve-fitting results in the region of 2000–400 cm−1 by employing the Gaussian-Lorentzian function. The curve-fitting analysis indicates the presence of typical stretching vibration of the graphene backbone chain (ν CC) at 1548 cm−1, along with the C-H asymmetric deformation vibration from sp3 hybridization (δ C–H) at 1456 cm−1, which reflects the defective nature of GQDs. Several oxygen functional groups are correlated with the structure of GQDs [36,37]. These include carboxyl group stretching (ν CO), as well as deformation (δ OH) and out-of-plane deformation (op OH) vibrations of the –OH group at 1730, 1359, and 934 cm−1, respectively. In addition, the dominant band at 1229 cm−1 could be assigned to the combination of OH deformation (δ OH) and C–O stretching (ν C–OH), including the C–O–C stretching vibration of epoxide groups (ν C–O–C) [36,38]. Finally, the vibrational frequency bands of ν C–O of carboxylic acids and alcohols are identified at 1120 and 1027 cm−1, respectively. Spectra of composites exhibit the characteristic vibrations of CeO2-δ (Ce–O and CeO), besides the GQDs skeletal vibrations (ν CC) at 1547 cm−1, which verify the presence of both components at composites. The Ce–O and CeO vibrational modes are located at 998, 911 cm−1, and 840 cm−1 [39], and Ce–O–Ce moieties frequencies are identified at 1339 and 1097 cm−1. The curve-fitting analysis indicates that the strong Ce–O bond frequency is centered at 365, 424, and 369 cm−1 for CeO2-GQD80, CeO2-GQD250, and CeO2-GQD400 composites, respectively. The (Ce-OH) and (Ce δ-OH) vibrational modes at 445 and 1390 cm−1 confirmed the presence of Ce3+ hydroxide as determined from XRD analysis [40]. The change in position of the broad band containing Ce–OH and Ce–O modes suggests a strong interaction between composite components [41]. In addition, the band at 1630 cm−1 was associated with the characteristic H–O–H (δ HOH) deformation vibration, indicating adsorbed water over CeO2‐GQD80 composites. The absence of H–O–H (δ HOH) vibrations in CeO2-GQD250 and CeO2-GQD400 composites suggests a dehydration process with the increase in temperature. Moreover, the band located at 1494 cm−1 was assigned to CO3 vibration, suggesting CO2 adsorbed on the surface of all CeO2-δ-GQD composites [42–44].

Fig. 6a depicts the C 1 s high-resolution spectra of pristine GQDs and CeO2-δ-GQD composites. The C 1 s spectrum for GQDs exhibits characteristic peaks at 288.9, 287.2, and 286.4 eV attributed to –O–CO, C–O, and CO groups, respectively. In addition, peaks at 285.5, 284.7, and 283.8 eV are assigned to C–C (sp3), CC (sp2), and C–Ca (amorphous carbon), respectively. The CeO2-GQD80 composites show a similar C 1 s spectrum to the GQDs, whereas those of CeO2-GQD250 and CeO2-GQD400 are strongly influenced by temperature. This indicates the successful preparation of composites either by preserving or modifying the C structural nature, thereby expanding their functionality. Heating at intermediate temperature leads to a considerable increment of new functional groups at CeO2-GQD250 composites by the effect of air atmosphere and breaking of CC bonds. Further, the remaining small graphitic domains at CeO2-GQD400 composites contain a large number of edge C atoms contributing to the sp3 signal.

The high-resolution O 1 s spectra of pristine GQDs and CeO2-δ-GQD composites are shown in Fig. 6b. The GQDs displayed four characteristic peaks at 535.6, 533.6, 532.2, and 530.9 eV, assigned to O–H, O–C, OC bonds, and quinones [45], respectively. The spectra of CeO2-δ-GQD composites exhibit, in addition, two peaks associated with O–Ce (III) (∼530.4 eV) and O–Ce (IV) (∼529.4 eV). It is noteworthy that Ce4+ is the dominant source of all composites. In particular, CeO2-GQD250 composites exhibit the lowest O–Ce (III) signal, implying fewer oxygen vacancies and less presence of Ce(OH)3. In contrast, CeO2-GQD400 composites show a notable O–Ce (III) band, similar to CeO2-GQD80 composites, possibly associated with the larger presence of Ce2O3. This suggests an important presence of oxygen vacancies at CeO2-GQD400 composites.

The XPS spectra from Ce 3 d core levels for CeO2-δ-GQD composites are depicted in Fig. 6c. The prominent Ce peaks from 3 d5/2 and 3 d3/2 levels (v and u) were observed at ∼882.7 and ∼901.1 eV, respectively. Both spin-orbit doublets displayed four corresponding satellite peaks (v0, v′, v″, v‴, u0, u′, u″, and u‴), indicating the presence of Ce4+ (solid line) and Ce3+ (broken line) at every composite. The XPS analysis showed a relatively higher proportion of Ce4+ at CeO2-GQD250 composites than the others (Table 1).

Fig. 7a depicts the UV-Vis absorption spectra of pristine GQDs, CeO2-δ-GQD composites, and reference CeO2NPs. The GQDs exhibit an intrinsic π→π * transition from CC bonds at 230 nm, as well as a maximum of absorption at 280 nm associated with the n→π * transition of CO bonds. Further, the broad absorption tail suggests the existence of defect states mainly contributed by oxygen-containing functional groups and structural defects, confirming the intrinsic nature of GQDs [46,47]. The CeO2NPs show a contrasting behavior in which strong absorbance starts below 450 nm and reaches a maximum at around 310 nm, corresponding to the electronic transfer between O 2p and Ce 4 f molecular orbitals [48]. The composites display an intermediate absorbance tendency with a dominant GQDs behavior for CeO2-GQD80 and CeO2-GQD250 composites and approaching that of CeO2NPs for CeO2-GQD400 composites. These behaviors reflect the close association between the highly defective GQDs and nanosized crystalline CeO2 areas with abundant oxygen vacancies. Specifically, the absorption edge of CeO2-GQD400 composites strongly shifts to UV due to a quantum size effect, as will be explained below. Further, their broad absorption tail might be associated with intrinsic oxygen vacancy defects at the CeO2 lattice component and 4 f-5 d transitions for Ce3+ [41,49]. These results could be supported by the highest Ce3+ content at CeO2-GQD 400 composites revealed from Raman and XPS analyses.

The band gaps, Eg, of pristine GQDs, CeO2-δ-GQD composites, and reference CeO2NPs, were estimated from Tauc’s relation considering direct O 2p-Ce 4 f transition [7,38,42]. Fig. 7b shows the (αhν)2 vs. hν plots. The determination of Eg for pristine GQDs is challenging, given its broad absorption profile [50]. Herein, the Eg of GQDs was estimated to be about 2.4 eV. Previous studies have indicated that the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups may decrease the Eg of graphene [51]. The Eg value for CeO2NPs was calculated to be about 3.2 eV, in agreement with earlier studies for bulk (3.1–3.2 eV) and nanosized CeO2 (3.3–3.6 eV) [7,38,42,48,52]. The component features strongly influence each Eg composite value. The Eg for CeO2-GQD80 composites (3.1 eV) is near that of CeO2NPs due to the relatively higher presence of oxygen-vacancies attributed to Ce(OH)3 and Ce2O3. In contrast, Eg for CeO2-GQD250 composites (2.7 eV) is approaching that of GQDs, probably related to a less presence of oxygen vacancies associated with vanishing Ce(OH)3 [22,41,48,52,53]. The Eg of CeO2-GQD400 composites is distinctively high (3.8 eV), despite the large Ce3+ presence. This may be interpreted as a merely CeO2-δ quantum size effect (∼2.9 nm) because of the extremely low presence of GQDs [7,42].

Fig. 8a shows the PL spectra of pristine GQDs, CeO2-δ-GQD composites, and reference CeO2NPs under excitation at 320 nm. All materials exhibit radiative emissions between 350 and 500 nm. The CeO2NPs display intense PL emission with a maximum of 365 nm, which is assigned to radiative relaxation of Ce 4 f promoted electrons (LUMO) from O 2p orbitals (HOMO) [7,42,52,54]. On the contrary, the pristine GQDs show weak PL emission from self-trapped excitons due to their conductive nature [22]. The PL emission of CeO2-δ-GQD composites turns broad and decreases in intensity, indicating a low recombination rate of charge carriers. The lowest PL emission emerges particularly from CeO2-GQD250 composites, which a synergistic interaction between components could explain. This effect may arise from the important presence of oxygen-containing groups at GQDs in combination with the relatively low Ce3+ content [22,52]. Excitation-dependent PL emission is depicted for CeO2-GQD250 composites in Fig. 8b. The CeO2-GQD250 composites exhibit a red-shift PL emission when excited from 300 to 370 nm. This behavior is associated with the size and shape of sp2 domains, O- and Ce- related surface states, and the great complexity of GQD defect states [26,51,55–57]. In summary, CeO2-GQD250 composites display a low recombination rate of charge carriers, which makes them highly attractive for photoelectrocatalysis, while CeO2-GQD400 composites show an advantageous high Ce3+ presence for catalytic applications.

We have demonstrated that novel 2D CeO2-δ-GQD composites can be synthesized by a simple LPD method followed by a thermal treatment in air at 250 and 400 °C. The composites consist of crystalline CeO2-δ regions of about 1.8–2.9 nm in size integrated into highly disordered GQD areas. The thermal treatment influences the GQD fraction, CeO2 crystal size, Ce3+/Ce4+ ratio, and O vacancies at composites. The CeO2-GQD250 composites exhibit a low recombination rate of charge carriers, while CeO2-GQD400 composites show an advantageous high Ce3+ presence besides high a Eg of 3.8 eV. These properties make the CeO2-δ-GQD composites highly attractive candidates to assist catalytic or photocatalytic reactions involving oxygen.

S. Santana-Hernandez: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing - original draft. M.A. Hernandez-Perez: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. M. Villanueva-Ibáñez: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. F. Chen: Supervision. Q. Shen: Supervision. R. Tu: Supervision. J.R. Vargas-Garcia: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. L.M. Zhang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Santana-Hernandez acknowledges "CONACYT", "México" the financial support for doctoral studies.
